cards.grammarNote: 을/를 marks the direct object — what the verb acts on. 을 after a consonant, 를 after a vowel. In casual spoken Korean it's often dropped, but it's used in writing and for clarity or emphasis.
너 = you (informal). 를 = object marker (vowel-ending noun). 사랑하다 → 사랑해요. 너를 사랑해 is more common in casual/romantic speech (dropping 요).
느낌 = feeling. 을 = object marker (consonant). 싫어하다 (to hate/dislike) → 싫어해요. 좋아해요 = like, 싫어해요 = dislike.
감사 = gratitude. 를 = object marker. 느끼다 (to feel) → 느껴요. 감사를 느끼다 = to feel gratitude. More literary; 감사해요 is more natural in speech.
나 = I/me (informal). 를 = object marker. 이해하다 (to understand) → 이해해요. 나를 이해해요? = Do you understand me?

cards.front: I miss the feeling of being happy.
cards.grammarNote: 기억 = memory. 을 = object marker (consonant). 그리워하다 (to miss/long for) → 그리워해요. 기억을 그리워하다 = to miss a memory.
약속 = promise. 을 = object marker. 지키다 (to keep/protect) → 지켜요. 약속을 지키다 = to keep a promise. 약속을 어기다 = to break a promise.
네가 = you (subject, 이/가 marker). 보다 + 고 싶다 = want to see / miss. In this structure, 가 marks the person missed as subject. 너를 보고 싶다 is also possible.
위로 = comfort/consolation. 를 = object marker. 받다 (to receive) → 받았어요 (past tense). 위로를 받다 = to receive comfort.
상처 = wound/hurt. 를 = object marker. 상처를 받다 = to be hurt (emotionally). 상처를 주다 = to hurt someone (to give a wound).
감사를 전하다 = to convey gratitude. 전하다 → 전해요. 를 = object marker (vowel-ending noun). More formal than just 감사해요.
이곳 = this place. 을 = object marker. 좋아하다 (to like) → 좋아해요. Note: 좋아해요 (I like it) vs 좋아요 (it is good/I like it as adjective verb).
